input.background: A $t$ test calculates $t_{\text {stat }}=6.60$. Assuming the study had more than just a few observations, you do not need a $t$ table or software utility to draw a conclusion about the test.
input.question: What is this conclusion, and why is a look-up table unnecessary?

output.answer: When the sample contains more than just a few observations, the associated $t$ statistic will have more than a few $d f$ and will look very much like a Standard Normal 2 distribution. Based on the 68-95-99.7 rule, we would almost never get a test statistic that is 6.6 standard deviations away from the 0 . Therefore, we can say that the $t$ test statistic is in the far right-hand tail of the sampling distribution and the $P$-value will be very, very small (e.g.. less than 0.01 ).

t_stat.description: The calculated t-statistic value from the t-test.

sample_size.value: more than just a few observations
sample_size.description: The sample size of the study, which is stated to be sufficiently large.
